revision.comment: 周防国の惣社。神道には明るくないので、詳しい方、手直しお願いします。
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 佐波神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Saba Shrine (Hōfu, Yamaguchi).jpg|200px]] |所在地 = 山口県防府市惣社町6番2号 | 緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 3|緯度秒 = 40.5 | 経度度 =131|経度分 =34|経度秒 = 57.2 |地図 = Japan Yamaguchi |祭神 = |神体 = |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = |本殿 = |別名 = 惣社金切社（旧称） |札所等 = |例祭 = [[4月2日]] |神事 = }} '''佐波神社'''（さばじんじゃ）は、[[山口県]][[防府市]]惣社町にある[[神社]]である。
== 歴史 == 多々良山の南麓に鎮座し、[[天照大神]]・[[素盞嗚尊]]・[[三穂津姫|三穂津姫命]]・[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命]]・級長津姫命・[[ウカノミタマ|倉稲魂命]]・[[住吉三神|底筒男命・中筒男命・表筒男命]]・[[大国主|大己貴命]]・[[事代主]]・[[タケミナカタ|建御名方神]]・[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌神]]・[[経津主神]]の十四神を祀る。
かつては惣社金切社といい、[[延喜式神名帳]]への記載がない式外神社であった。[[仲哀天皇]]と[[神功皇后]]が[[熊襲]]親征の際に戦勝祈願のために諸神を祀り、惣社宮となった。その後の[[三韓征伐]]の際に、[[三韓]]は[[陰陽五行思想]]によると「金」の方角にあたり、金を切り平げたと言う意味で金切社と称するようになったと伝えられている<ref name="Hofu1-196">『防府市史上巻』p196-197</ref>。
[[国司]]が赴任した際に、その国中の神社に参詣するしきたりであったが、容易なことではないため、[[国府]]にその国の諸神を祀る惣社（[[総社]]）を建立する制度ができた。このことから、建立時期は不詳であるが[[周防国衙跡|周防国府]]設立後と考えられている<ref name="Hofu1-196"/>
[[1907年]]（[[明治]]40年）6月3日、金切社に村社の勝間神社・日吉神社・八幡宮を合併し佐波神社と改称。[[1922年]]（[[大正]]11年）4月29日に県社に列し、[[1924年]]5月2日には神饌幣帛料供進社に指定された。この年に社殿を改築している<ref>『続防府市史』p244</ref>
境内には、防府市出身の作曲家[[大村能章]]の顕彰碑と歌碑があり、毎年[[1月23日]]には命日祭が行われる<ref>{{Cite web |date= |url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author = 御園生翁甫|date = 1956-3-20|title =防府市史上巻|publisher= 防府市教育委員会|pages = 196-197}} * {{Cite book|和書|author = 御園生翁甫|date = 1960-11-6|title = 続防府市史|publisher= 続防府市史刊行会|pages = 244-246}}

[[1907年]]（[[明治]]40年）6月3日、金切社に村社の勝間神社・日吉神社・八幡宮を合併し佐波神社と改称。[[1922年]]（[[大正]]11年）4月29日に県社に列し、[[1924年]]5月2日には神饌幣帛料供進社に指定された。この年に社殿を改築している<ref>『続防府市史』p244</ref>

revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deities = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden (main sanctuary) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha (sub-shrine for the Inari deity) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), torii.jpg|Torii (shrine gate) == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=防府市史上巻|publisher=防府市教育委員会|date=1956-03-20|pages=196–197}} * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=続防府市史|publisher=続防府市史刊行会|date=1960-11-06|pages=244–246}} == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Saba-jinja (Hofu)}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-substub|pref=山口県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さばしんじゃ}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Hōfu]] [[Category:Historical sites in Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines]] [[Category:Sōja (Shinto)]] [[Category:Suō Province|Shrine 0]]

Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden (main sanctuary) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha (sub-shrine for the Inari deity) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), torii.jpg|Torii (shrine gate) == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=防府市史上巻|publisher=防府市教育委員会|date=1956-03-20|pages=196–197}} * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=続防府市史|publisher=続防府市史刊行会|date=1960-11-06|pages=244–246}} == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Saba-jinja (Hofu)}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-substub|pref=山口県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さばしんじゃ}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Hōfu]] [[Category:Historical sites in Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines]] [[Category:Sōja (Shinto)]] [[Category:Suō Province|Shrine 0]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[category:original pages]]

Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden (main sanctuary) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha (sub-shrine for the Inari deity) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), torii.jpg|Torii (shrine gate) == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=防府市史上巻|publisher=防府市教育委員会|date=1956-03-20|pages=196–197}} * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=続防府市史|publisher=続防府市史刊行会|date=1960-11-06|pages=244–246}} == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Saba-jinja (Hofu)}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-substub|pref=山口県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さばしんじゃ}} [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Hōfu]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Historical sites in Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Former prefectural shrines]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Suō Province|Shrine 0]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[category:original pages]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[category:original pages]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[category:original pages]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden (main sanctuary) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha (sub-shrine for the Inari deity) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), torii.jpg|Torii (shrine gate) == book|author=御園生翁甫|title=続防府市史|publisher=続防府市史刊行会|date=1960-11-06|pages=244–246}} == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Saba-jinja (Hofu)}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-substub|pref=山口県}} revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden (main sanctuary) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha (sub-shrine for the Inari deity) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), torii.jpg|Torii (shrine gate) == book|author=御園生翁甫|title=続防府市史|publisher=続防府市史刊行会|date=1960-11-06|pages=244–246}} == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Saba-jinja (Hofu)}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-substub|pref=山口県}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden (main sanctuary) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha (sub-shrine for the Inari deity) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), torii.jpg|Torii (shrine gate) == book|author=御園生翁甫|title=続防府市史|publisher=続防府市史刊行会|date=1960-11-06|pages=244–246}} == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Saba-jinja (Hofu)}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-substub|pref=山口県}} The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. revision.comment: trimming down the excessive presence of categories from erroneous implementations
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden (main sanctuary) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha (sub-shrine for the Inari deity) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), torii.jpg|Torii (shrine gate) == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=防府市史上巻|publisher=防府市教育委員会|date=1956-03-20|pages=196–197}} * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=続防府市史|publisher=続防府市史刊行会|date=1960-11-06|pages=244–246}} == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Saba-jinja (Hofu)}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-substub|pref=山口県}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden (main sanctuary) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha (sub-shrine for the Inari deity) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), torii.jpg|Torii (shrine gate) == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=防府市史上巻|publisher=防府市教育委員会|date=1956-03-20|pages=196–197}} * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=続防府市史|publisher=続防府市史刊行会|date=1960-11-06|pages=244–246}} == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Saba-jinja (Hofu)}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-substub|pref=山口県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さばしんじゃ}} [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Hōfu]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Historical sites in Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Suō Province|Shrine 0]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Images]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden (main sanctuary) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha (sub-shrine for the Inari deity) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), torii.jpg|Torii (shrine gate) == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=防府市史上巻|publisher=防府市教育委員会|date=1956-03-20|pages=196–197}} * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=続防府市史|publisher=続防府市史刊行会|date=1960-11-06|pages=244–246}} == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Saba-jinja (Hofu)}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-substub|pref=山口県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さばしんじゃ}} [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Hōfu]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Historical sites in Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Suō Province|Shrine 0]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Images]] [[Category:bump 1-20]]

Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden (main sanctuary) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha (sub-shrine for the Inari deity) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), torii.jpg|Torii (shrine gate) == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=防府市史上巻|publisher=防府市教育委員会|date=1956-03-20|pages=196–197}} * {{Cite book|author=御園生翁甫|title=続防府市史|publisher=続防府市史刊行会|date=1960-11-06|pages=244–246}} == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Saba-jinja (Hofu)}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-substub|pref=山口県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さばしんじゃ}} [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Hōfu]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Historical sites in Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Suō Province|Shrine 0]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Saba Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Images]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden (main sanctuary) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha (sub-shrine for the Inari deity) File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), torii.jpg|Torii (shrine gate) == In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Saba Shrine | image = [[File:Saba-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds == Haiden (worship hall) | location = 6-2 Sōja-chō, Hōfu, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|03|40.5|N|131|34|57.2|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi | deity = | shrine_rank = [[Suō Province]] [[Sōja]]
Former [[Kensha]] (Prefectural Shrine) | established= | architecture= | alternate_names = Sōja Kanekiri-sha | festivals = [[April 2]] | rites = }} '''Saba Shrine''' (佐波神社, ''Saba Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Sōja-chō, [[Hōfu]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]]. It served as the [[Sōja]] (provincial shrine) of [[Suō Province]] and was classified as a [[Kensha]] (prefectural shrine) under the [[modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == History == Situated at the southern foot of Tatara Mountain, the shrine venerates a pantheon of fourteen deities, including [[Amaterasu]], [[Susanoo]], [[Mihotsuhime|Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto]], [[Shinatsuhiko]], Shinatsuhime, [[Ukano Mitama|Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto]], [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] (Sokotsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Uwatsutsuo), [[Ōkuninushi]], [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], [[Takemikazuchi]], and [[Futsunushi-no-Kami]]. Originally known as ''Sōja Kanekiri-sha,'' the shrine was not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' and is classified as an [[Shikigai Shrine|unranked shrine]]. According to legend, Emperor [[Chūai]] and Empress [[Jingū]] enshrined the gods here to pray for victory during their campaign against the [[Kumaso]]. The name ''Kanekiri'' ("metal cutting") is thought to symbolize the act of pacifying (cutting down) resistance during the [[Three Han Campaign|expedition to the Three Han]] regions, reflecting the [[Yin-Yang Five Phases|Five Elements theory]] that associates Korea with the "metal" direction御園生翁甫編『防府市史上巻』pp. 196–197. As a [[Sōja]], the shrine was established as a central place for provincial worship where the deities of all local shrines could be venerated. The exact date of its establishment is unclear but is presumed to have been after the founding of the [[Suō Provincial Capital|provincial capital]]. In 1907, the shrine merged with the village shrines [[Katsuma Shrine]], [[Hiyoshi Shrine]], and [[Hachiman Shrine]], adopting the name ''Saba Shrine.'' In 1922, it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine, and by 1924 it was designated to receive offerings from the state. Renovations to the shrine buildings were carried out during this period御園生翁甫編『続防府市史』pp. 244–246. The shrine grounds feature a memorial and song monument dedicated to the composer [[Nōshō Ōmura]], a Hōfu native. A commemorative festival is held annually on [[January 23]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.kanko-hofu.gr.jp/course/oomuranoushou|title=作曲家大村能章ゆかりのコース|publisher=防府市観光協会|accessdate=2017-07-08}}. == Grounds ==
